name: Nobel Prize Facts & Laureates
description: Nobel Prize categories, notable winners, and key facts about the world's most prestigious awards

questions.question: Who established the Nobel Prizes?
questions.explanation: Swedish chemist, engineer, and inventor Alfred Nobel established the prizes in his 1895 will, allocating 94% of his fortune -- about 31 million Swedish kronor -- to fund them. Nobel held 355 patents during his lifetime and is best known for inventing dynamite in 1867. The prizes were first awarded in 1901, five years after his death. Legend has it that a French newspaper prematurely published his obituary calling him a 'merchant of death,' motivating him to leave a more positive legacy.

questions.question: How many Nobel Prize categories are there?
questions.explanation: There are six categories: Physics, Chemistry, Physiology or Medicine, Literature, Peace, and Economic Sciences. However, only five were established by Alfred Nobel's will in 1895. The sixth -- the Prize in Economic Sciences -- was created in 1968 by Sweden's central bank and first awarded in 1969. Purists sometimes argue there are only five 'real' Nobel Prizes since Economics was added later, but it is administered by the same institution and awarded at the same ceremony.

questions.question: In what year were the first Nobel Prizes awarded?
questions.explanation: The first Nobel Prizes were awarded on December 10, 1901, the fifth anniversary of Alfred Nobel's death. The inaugural laureates were Wilhelm Rontgen (Physics, for discovering X-rays), Jacobus Henricus van 't Hoff (Chemistry), Emil von Behring (Physiology or Medicine, for developing a diphtheria antitoxin), Sully Prudhomme (Literature), and Henry Dunant and Frederic Passy (Peace). December 10 has remained the date of the award ceremony ever since.

questions.question: What is the Nobel Prize in Economics officially called?
questions.answer: Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel
alternateAnswers: The Sveriges Riksbank Prize in Economic Sciences in Memory of Alfred Nobel
questions.explanation: The Economics prize was not part of Alfred Nobel's original 1895 will. Sweden's central bank, Sveriges Riksbank, established it in 1968 to celebrate the bank's 300th anniversary, and it was first awarded in 1969 to Ragnar Frisch and Jan Tinbergen. Though it is administered by the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences and laureates receive their awards at the same Stockholm ceremony, Nobel's descendants have occasionally objected to it being called a 'Nobel Prize.'

questions.question: What city hosts the Nobel Peace Prize ceremony?
questions.explanation: The Nobel Peace Prize is awarded in Oslo, Norway, at Oslo City Hall, while all other Nobel Prizes are awarded in Stockholm, Sweden. Alfred Nobel specified this arrangement in his will but never explained why. At the time, Norway and Sweden were in a political union under the Swedish crown, and some historians believe Nobel wanted Norway to play a role to strengthen ties between the two countries. The union dissolved in 1905, but the tradition continued.

questions.question: In what city are most Nobel Prize ceremonies held?
questions.explanation: Five of the six Nobel Prize categories -- Physics, Chemistry, Physiology or Medicine, Literature, and Economic Sciences -- are awarded in Stockholm, Sweden. The award ceremony takes place at the Stockholm Concert Hall, followed by a lavish banquet at Stockholm City Hall. The sole exception is the Peace Prize, which is awarded in Oslo, Norway. All ceremonies take place on December 10, the anniversary of Alfred Nobel's death in 1896.

questions.question: On what date are the Nobel Prizes awarded each year?
questions.explanation: Nobel Prizes are awarded every year on December 10, the anniversary of Alfred Nobel's death in 1896. The ceremonies in both Stockholm and Oslo take place on this date. Nobel died in Sanremo, Italy, after suffering a stroke at the age of 63. The date has been observed consistently since the first prizes were handed out in 1901, making it one of the most fixed dates on the international cultural calendar.

questions.question: Who is the only person to win Nobel Prizes in two different sciences?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903 (shared with Pierre Curie and Henri Becquerel for research on radioactivity) and the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1911 (for discovering radium and polonium). She was Polish-born and later became a French citizen. Linus Pauling also won two Nobel Prizes, but in Chemistry (1954) and Peace (1962) -- only one of which is a science category. Curie remains unique in spanning two scientific disciplines.

questions.question: Who won the Nobel Peace Prize in 1964 for civil rights leadership?
questions.explanation: Martin Luther King Jr. received the 1964 Nobel Peace Prize at age 35 for combating racial inequality through nonviolent resistance. At the time he was the youngest man to receive the Peace Prize. King led the Montgomery Bus Boycott, delivered the famous 'I Have a Dream' speech at the 1963 March on Washington, and was instrumental in passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. He donated the $54,123 prize money to the civil rights movement.

questions.question: What physicist won the 1921 Nobel Prize for the photoelectric effect?
questions.explanation: Albert Einstein received the 1921 Nobel Prize in Physics (actually awarded in 1922) 'for his services to Theoretical Physics, and especially for his discovery of the law of the photoelectric effect.' Contrary to popular belief, he did not win for his theory of relativity -- some members of the Nobel Committee remained skeptical of general relativity at the time. His photoelectric effect work helped establish quantum mechanics by showing that light behaves as discrete packets of energy called photons.

questions.question: Who is the youngest Nobel laureate?
questions.explanation: Malala Yousafzai of Pakistan won the Nobel Peace Prize in 2014 at the age of 17, making her the youngest Nobel laureate in history. She shared the prize with Indian children's rights activist Kailash Satyarthi. Malala gained international attention after being shot in the head by a Taliban gunman in 2012 for advocating girls' education in Pakistan's Swat Valley. She survived and became a global symbol of the fight for children's right to education.

questions.question: Who is the oldest Nobel laureate, winning at age 97?
questions.explanation: American physicist John B. Goodenough won the 2019 Nobel Prize in Chemistry at the age of 97, making him the oldest person ever to receive a Nobel Prize. He shared the prize with M. Stanley Whittingham and Akira Yoshino for the development of lithium-ion batteries. Goodenough's key contribution was identifying cobalt oxide as the cathode material that made rechargeable lithium-ion batteries practical. These batteries now power smartphones, laptops, and electric vehicles worldwide.

questions.question: What musician won the 2016 Nobel Prize in Literature?
questions.explanation: American singer-songwriter Bob Dylan received the 2016 Nobel Prize in Literature 'for having created new poetic expressions within the great American song tradition.' The award was controversial because Dylan is primarily a musician, not a traditional author, sparking debate about whether song lyrics qualify as literature. Dylan did not attend the December ceremony in Stockholm, sending a speech to be read on his behalf instead. He eventually collected his medal and diploma in a private meeting in 2017.

questions.question: Which French philosopher declined the Nobel Prize in Literature in 1964?
questions.explanation: Jean-Paul Sartre declined the 1964 Nobel Prize in Literature, stating that he systematically refused all official honors because accepting such distinctions would compromise his independence as a writer. Sartre was the leading figure of existentialism and wrote novels, plays, and philosophical works including 'Being and Nothingness' and 'Nausea.' He remains one of only a handful of people to voluntarily turn down a Nobel Prize. The Swedish Academy still lists him as a laureate despite his refusal.

questions.question: Which Vietnamese diplomat declined the Nobel Peace Prize in 1973?
questions.explanation: Le Duc Tho declined the 1973 Nobel Peace Prize, which he was jointly awarded with US Secretary of State Henry Kissinger for negotiating the Paris Peace Accords to end American involvement in the Vietnam War. Le Duc Tho refused the prize because peace had not actually been achieved in Vietnam -- fighting continued despite the accords. He is the only person to voluntarily decline the Nobel Peace Prize. Kissinger accepted his share but was also controversial, with two Nobel Committee members resigning in protest.

questions.question: Who is the only person to win two unshared Nobel Prizes?
questions.explanation: American chemist Linus Pauling won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry in 1954 for his research on chemical bonds and the structure of molecules, and the Nobel Peace Prize in 1962 for his campaign against nuclear weapons testing. He is the only individual to win two unshared Nobel Prizes (Marie Curie shared her Physics prize). Pauling's peace activism began after World War II when he circulated a petition signed by over 11,000 scientists urging an end to nuclear testing, contributing to the 1963 Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.

questions.question: What explosive did Alfred Nobel invent that made him wealthy enough to fund the Nobel Prizes?
questions.explanation: Alfred Nobel invented dynamite in 1867 by combining nitroglycerin with diatomaceous earth, creating a stable explosive far safer to handle than raw nitroglycerin. The invention revolutionized mining, construction, and demolition, making Nobel enormously wealthy. He also invented gelignite (1875) and ballistite (1887). The military applications of his inventions troubled Nobel, and he reportedly wanted to be remembered for more than destruction -- which motivated his decision to fund the prizes that bear his name.

questions.question: What organization has won the Nobel Peace Prize three times?
questions.answer: International Committee of the Red Cross
questions.explanation: The International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1917, 1944, and 1963 -- more than any other organization. The 1917 and 1944 prizes recognized its humanitarian work during World War I and World War II respectively, particularly its efforts to visit prisoners of war and facilitate communication between separated families. The 1963 prize was shared with the League of Red Cross Societies, marking the centenary of the ICRC's founding by Henry Dunant in 1863.

questions.question: What family holds the record for the most Nobel Prizes, with five laureates across the family?
questions.explanation: The Curie family accumulated an extraordinary five Nobel Prizes. Marie Curie won two (Physics 1903, Chemistry 1911), her husband Pierre Curie shared the 1903 Physics prize, and their daughter Irene Joliot-Curie and her husband Frederic Joliot-Curie shared the 1935 Chemistry prize for discovering artificial radioactivity. Additionally, Henry Labouisse, husband of Marie's second daughter Eve, accepted the 1965 Nobel Peace Prize on behalf of UNICEF as the organization's director.

questions.question: What was Alfred Nobel's nationality?
questions.explanation: Alfred Nobel was born in Stockholm, Sweden, on October 21, 1833. Though Swedish by birth, he was remarkably cosmopolitan -- he lived in Russia as a child, studied chemistry in Paris, and spent significant time in Germany and Italy. He died in Sanremo, Italy, on December 10, 1896, at age 63. Nobel spoke five languages fluently: Swedish, Russian, English, French, and German. Despite his international life, his prizes remain closely associated with Swedish institutions.

questions.question: Which British biochemist won the Nobel Prize in Chemistry twice, in 1958 and 1980?
questions.explanation: Frederick Sanger is one of only two people to win the Nobel Prize in the same category twice (the other being John Bardeen in Physics). Sanger's 1958 prize recognized his work on the structure of the insulin protein, where he determined its complete amino acid sequence. His 1980 prize, shared with Paul Berg and Walter Gilbert, was for developing methods to determine the base sequences in DNA. His DNA sequencing technique became foundational for the Human Genome Project.

questions.question: Which American physicist won the Nobel Prize in Physics twice, in 1956 and 1972?
questions.explanation: John Bardeen is the only person to win the Nobel Prize in Physics twice. His 1956 prize, shared with William Shockley and Walter Brattain, was for the invention of the transistor -- arguably the most important invention of the 20th century, enabling all modern electronics. His 1972 prize, shared with Leon Cooper and John Robert Schrieffer, was for developing the BCS theory of superconductivity. Bardeen was famously modest and unassuming despite his extraordinary achievements.

questions.question: Who won the very first Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901, for discovering X-rays?
questions.explanation: German physicist Wilhelm Rontgen received the inaugural Nobel Prize in Physics in 1901 for his discovery of X-rays in 1895. Rontgen famously produced an X-ray image of his wife Anna's hand, clearly showing her bones and wedding ring. He refused to take out patents on his discovery, believing it should benefit humanity freely. His discovery revolutionized medicine by allowing doctors to see inside the body without surgery. The unit of radiation exposure, the roentgen, is named after him.

questions.question: Who was the first woman to win the Nobel Peace Prize, in 1905?
questions.explanation: Austrian pacifist Bertha von Suttner won the 1905 Nobel Peace Prize, becoming the first woman to receive the honor. She was a novelist and activist whose 1889 anti-war novel 'Lay Down Your Arms' became a landmark of the peace movement. Remarkably, she had a personal connection to Alfred Nobel -- she briefly worked as his secretary in 1876, and their subsequent correspondence is believed to have influenced his decision to include a peace prize in his will.

questions.question: Watson, Crick, and Wilkins won the 1962 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine for discovering what?
questions.explanation: James Watson, Francis Crick, and Maurice Wilkins received the 1962 Nobel Prize for determining the double-helix structure of DNA. Their famous 1953 paper proposed that DNA consists of two intertwined strands in a helical shape, with complementary base pairs holding them together. The contribution of Rosalind Franklin, whose X-ray crystallography data was crucial to the discovery, went unrecognized by the Nobel Committee. Franklin had died of ovarian cancer in 1958, and Nobel Prizes are not awarded posthumously.

questions.question: Which nun and missionary won the 1979 Nobel Peace Prize for humanitarian work in India?
questions.explanation: Mother Teresa, born Anjeze Gonxhe Bojaxhiu in what is now North Macedonia, won the 1979 Nobel Peace Prize for her decades of work among the poorest of the poor in Calcutta (now Kolkata), India. She founded the Missionaries of Charity in 1950, which grew to operate hospices, orphanages, and schools worldwide. She was canonized as a saint by the Catholic Church in 2016. Her Nobel acceptance is notable for its simplicity -- she requested the traditional banquet be canceled and the funds given to the poor.

questions.question: Who shared the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize with F.W. de Klerk for ending apartheid in South Africa?
questions.explanation: Nelson Mandela shared the 1993 Nobel Peace Prize with South African President F.W. de Klerk for their work in peacefully ending the apartheid system of racial segregation. Mandela had been imprisoned for 27 years (1964-1990) for his anti-apartheid activism before being released and negotiating with de Klerk's government. He went on to become South Africa's first Black president in 1994. The joint award recognized that both sides chose negotiation over continued conflict.

questions.question: Which American author won the 1954 Nobel Prize in Literature, known for 'The Old Man and the Sea'?
questions.explanation: Ernest Hemingway received the 1954 Nobel Prize in Literature 'for his mastery of the art of narrative, most recently demonstrated in The Old Man and the Sea, and for the influence that he has exerted on contemporary style.' Hemingway's spare, direct prose style revolutionized modern fiction. He did not attend the ceremony in Stockholm, citing injuries from two plane crashes in Africa earlier that year. His telegram to the Swedish Academy was characteristically brief and gracious.

questions.question: Since 1974, Nobel Prizes cannot be awarded posthumously. Which UN Secretary-General was one of the last posthumous recipients, winning the Peace Prize in 1961?
questions.explanation: Swedish diplomat Dag Hammarskjold was awarded the 1961 Nobel Peace Prize after his death in a plane crash in September 1961 while on a peacekeeping mission to the Congo. He served as the second Secretary-General of the United Nations from 1953 until his death. The Nobel Foundation changed its rules in 1974 to prohibit posthumous awards, with an exception only if the laureate dies between the announcement and the ceremony. Hammarskjold remains one of only two people to receive a posthumous Nobel Prize.

questions.question: Nobel Prize laureates receive a gold medal, a diploma, and what else?
questions.explanation: Each Nobel Prize comes with three components: a gold medal, a personalized diploma, and a substantial monetary award. The prize money has varied over the years and is currently set at 11 million Swedish kronor (roughly $1 million USD) per prize category. If multiple laureates share a prize, the money is divided among them. The funds come from the investment returns on Alfred Nobel's original endowment. The 18-karat gold medal weighs about 175 grams and features a portrait of Alfred Nobel.
